name: Post-Quantum Key Exchange
overview: TrustDB's cryptographic layer uses ML-KEM-1024 (NIST FIPS 203) hybridized with X25519 for key exchange, aligned with CNSA 2.0 requirements. The Cryptographic Agility Module (CAM) allows runtime algorithm selection and graceful migration, ensuring the system can adopt new post-quantum standards without data re-encryption or downtime.

howItWorks.title: Hybrid Key Exchange
howItWorks.description: ML-KEM-1024 (lattice-based, NIST Level 5) is combined with X25519 (elliptic curve) in a hybrid construction. Both must succeed for key establishment — this provides quantum resistance while maintaining classical security guarantees.

howItWorks.title: CAM Algorithm Registry
howItWorks.description: The Cryptographic Agility Module maintains a registry of supported algorithms with version tags. Each encrypted page records which algorithm version was used, enabling mixed-version reads during migration periods.

howItWorks.title: CNSA 2.0 Alignment
howItWorks.description: Algorithm choices follow NSA CNSA 2.0 guidance: ML-KEM for key encapsulation, ML-DSA (Dilithium) for signatures, SHA-384+ for hashing. This positions TrustDB for government and defense use cases.

howItWorks.title: Graceful Migration
howItWorks.description: When a new algorithm is added to the registry, new writes use the updated algorithm while reads transparently handle both old and new. Background re-encryption can optionally upgrade legacy pages during compaction.

comparisons.narrative: Most databases have no post-quantum cryptography story at all. Those that use TLS 1.3 may inherit PQ from the transport layer, but this does not protect data at rest. TrustDB's approach is unique in embedding PQ key exchange into the storage layer itself, with cryptographic agility for future algorithm changes. The hybrid approach adds ~130µs over classical X25519 alone, which is negligible for page-level key establishment.

competitors.approach: Relies on OpenSSL/TLS for transport encryption; TDE uses AES with classical key management
competitors.limitation: No post-quantum at-rest encryption; migration requires full re-encryption

competitors.approach: TLS 1.3 for transport; CSFLE with classical RSA/AES
competitors.limitation: No PQ key exchange for field-level encryption; no algorithm agility
No PQ support; key rotation requires coordinated cluster operation
PQ depends on AWS KMS roadmap; no customer control over algorithm selection
